blocks.text: In the eyes of the foolish they seemed to have died, and their departure was thought to be a disaster,
and their going from us to be their destruction; but they are at peace.
For though in the sight of others they were punished, their hope is full of immortality.
4 : rather than bodily resurrection reflects Greek influence (1.15; 2.23; 4.1; 6.18–19; 8.13,17; 12.1; 15.3; cf. 2 Esd 2.45; 7.13,96; 8.54; 4 Macc 9.22).
Having been disciplined a little, they will receive great good, because God tested them and found them worthy of himself;
like gold in the furnace he tried them, and like a sacrificial burnt offering he accepted them.
In the time of their visitation they will shine forth, and will run like sparks through the stubble.
They will govern nations and rule over peoples, and the Lord will reign over them forever.
7–8 : , in apocalyptic literature the glory of the virtuous is often described as starlike brilliance (e.g., Dan 12.3; Matt 13.43) and as rulership (e.g., Dan 7.27).
Those who trust in him will understand truth, and the faithful will abide with him in love, because grace and mercy are upon his holy ones, and he watches over his elect.
9 : , , in Wisdom, the reward of immortality is fashioned more from the covenantal promises to Israel than from Greek philosophy.
But the ungodly will be punished as their reasoning deserves, those who disregarded the righteous and rebelled against the Lord;
for those who despise wisdom and instruction are miserable. Their hope is vain, their labors are unprofitable, and their works are useless.
Their wives are foolish, and their children evil;
their offspring are accursed. For blessed is the barren woman who is undefiled, who has not entered into a sinful union; she will have fruit when God examines souls.

3.1–13a: The first diptych: the just are in the hand of God. The first diptych counters the final judgment of the wicked against the just who are tested with a shameful death (2.12–20). Only from the perspective of the ungodly is the death of the virtuous considered a tragedy. In reality, they had been tested by God and found worthy (cf. Deut 8.2–5; Ps 17.3).

10–13a : The connection between the way one sins and the way one is punished is a principle which will be applied in the plague narrative of the Exodus from Egypt (11.16; 12.23–27; 14.30–31). A similar relationship appears in several psalms of lament (e.g., Pss 7.15–16; 9.15; 35.8; 37.14–15; 141.10). wives and offspring, these concluding images of judgment introduce the next diptych which contrasts the apparent lack of fruit of the virtuous with the apparent abundant fruit of the ungodly.

Blessed also is the eunuch whose hands have done no lawless deed, and who has not devised wicked things against the Lord; for special favor will be shown him for his faithfulness, and a place of great delight in the temple of the Lord.
For the fruit of good labors is renowned, and the root of understanding does not fail.

13b–15 : The lack of children was often considered a grave misfortune if not a curse (cf. Gen 30.23; 1 Sam 1.4–8). The eunuch was barred from both the priesthood and the assembly of the Lord (cf. Lev 21.20; Deut 23.2). Wisdom overturns these judgments on the basis of virtue. The virtuous barren woman will have much fruit at the time of God's visitation (cf. Isa 54.1), and the eunuch who has avoided evil will have a place in the Temple (cf. Isa 56.3–4).
But children of adulterers will not come to maturity, and the offspring of an unlawful union will perish.
Even if they live long they will be held of no account, and finally their old age will be without honor.
If they die young, they will have no hope and no consolation on the day of judgment.
For the end of an unrighteous generation is grievous.
1.1–6.21: Exhortation to love righteousness. This opening section embodies a complex argument for justice in an elegant concentric structure:
3.1–4.20: In defense of virtue and justice. In three diptychs the author contrasts the reality of immortal life for the righteous with the mere appearance of power in the life of the ungodly.

3.13b–4.6: The second diptych: the moral strength of the virtuous. This central diptych counters the argument of the ungodly, who declared their might to be their law of right and what is weak to be useless (2.10–11).
16–19 : In contrast, will have a end. The illicit gains of injustice are ephemeral (cf. Job 20).
